status.state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
status.product_description: Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X) communication stack implementing DSRC and C-V2X for intersection collision warning, emergency vehicle preemption, and platooning. SAE J2735/J3161.

plan.description: Define stakeholder requirements for V2X use cases: intersection collision warning (ICW), emergency vehicle preemption (EVP), and cooperative adaptive cruise control / platooning. Map to SAE J2735 message types (BSM, SPAT, MAP, SRM, SSM, PVD) and J3161 application layer behaviors.
acceptance_criteria: Stakeholder requirement document produced with ≥30 numbered SRs
Every SR traced to at least one SAE J2735 message type or J3161 application
ICW, EVP, and platooning use cases each have scenario diagrams (happy path + 3 failure modes)
Latency budgets defined: ICW ≤100 ms E2E, EVP ≤50 ms, platooning gap ≤30 ms

plan.description: Conduct HARA (Hazard Analysis and Risk Assessment) per ISO 26262 Part 3 for all three V2X applications. Assign ASIL levels. Produce FMEA for message loss, spoofed BSM, GPS drift, and channel congestion failure modes. Generate fault tree for intersection collision warning false-negative.
acceptance_criteria: HARA table covers all identified hazards with S/E/C ratings and ASIL assignments
ASIL decomposition proposed for each safety goal
FMEA covers ≥20 failure modes including RF jamming, GPS spoofing, late packet delivery
FTA for ICW false-negative has ≤5 cut sets identified with probability estimates
Document reviewed and signed by safety_engineer and regulatory_specialist
ASIL level determined for each function
Fault tree analysis complete

plan.description: Produce Design History File skeleton and regulatory compliance matrix for UNECE R155 (cybersecurity), R156 (software update), and ISO 26262 ASIL decomposition evidence. Map every SR from Step 1 to a verification method (test, analysis, inspection). Generate traceability matrix SR→Safety Goal→Verification.
acceptance_criteria: DHF skeleton contains all mandatory ISO 26262 work products for ASIL-B/C
Traceability matrix covers 100% of SRs from Step 1
V&V plan defines entry/exit criteria for each verification phase
UNECE R155 cybersecurity management plan includes threat categories from SAE J3101
UNECE R156 SOTA (Software Over The Air) update procedure documented

plan.description: Threat model the V2X stack using STRIDE. Cover BSM spoofing/replay, SPAT signal hijacking, C-V2X PC5 Uu man-in-the-middle, certificate revocation latency, and misbehavior detection (IEEE 1609.2 / ETSI TS 102 941). Define SCMS (Security Credential Management System) integration points.
acceptance_criteria: STRIDE table covers all 6 categories for each attack surface
SCMS integration architecture diagram produced with certificate lifecycle flows
Misbehavior detection algorithm specified (local + global MBD thresholds)
SBOM generated listing all third-party libraries with CVE scan results
Security controls mapped to UNECE R155 Annex 5 threat categories

plan.description: Set up the development environment: AUTOSAR Classic/Adaptive toolchain (ARXML import), cross-compilation toolchain for target SoC (e.g., NXP S32G or Qualcomm 9150 C-V2X), Yocto-based Linux BSP for RSU, CMake superbuild, Git submodules for open-source WAVE stack (OpenV2X or similar), and Docker dev containers.
acceptance_criteria: Docker dev container builds successfully with all toolchains installed
cmake --build succeeds for all targets (OBU firmware, RSU Linux daemon, simulation harness)
AUTOSAR ARXML schema validation passes for base ECU extract
Cross-compilation produces ELF binary; arm-none-eabi-size output recorded as baseline
Pre-commit hooks enforce clang-format, cppcheck, and MISRA-C 2012 subset via pc-lint

plan.description: Implement the DSRC (IEEE 802.11p / WAVE) radio HAL layer: MLME/MAC SAP bindings, channel switching (CCH/SCH), EDCA QoS priority queues, and transmit power control. Target the Cohda MK6 or equivalent DSRC modem via SPI/PCIe HAL abstraction.
acceptance_criteria: HAL init/deinit cycle passes with no memory leaks (Valgrind/AddressSanitizer clean)
Channel switching latency measured ≤2 ms CCH→SCH (FreeRTOS task timing log)
EDCA queues verified: AC_VO packets preempt AC_BE under 80% channel load
Transmit power control API accepts dBm value and modem confirms via SPI ACK
Unit test coverage ≥85% for hal_dsrc.c (gcov report attached)
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement the C-V2X PC5 (sidelink) and Uu (cellular) HAL layer per 3GPP TS 36.213 / TS 38.885. Expose a unified radio abstraction API that allows the application layer to select DSRC or C-V2X at runtime. Include resource pool configuration, HARQ retransmission control, and SLSS synchronization.
acceptance_criteria: Unified radio API compiles for both DSRC and C-V2X without #ifdef pollution
PC5 resource pool configuration loads from NVM parameter set
SLSS synchronization acquires lock within 500 ms of cold start (test log)
Mode switching (DSRC↔C-V2X) completes in ≤10 ms measured via oscilloscope/GPIO toggle
HARQ retransmission reduces PER from baseline 15% to ≤5% in 6 dB SNR test
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement WAVE / ITS-G5 protocol stack layers above the radio HAL: IEEE 1609.3 WAVE Short Message Protocol (WSMP), 1609.2 security header encoding/decoding (pseudonym certificates, digital signatures via ECDSA P-256), GeoNetworking (ETSI EN 302 636-4-1), and BTP transport (ETSI EN 302 636-5-1).
acceptance_criteria: WSMP encode/decode roundtrip test passes for all PSID values in SAE J2735
ECDSA sign + verify cycle ≤2 ms on target SoC (measured with hardware timer)
GeoNetworking GeoBroadcast packet forwarding verified in 3-hop simulated topology
BTP port demultiplexing correctly routes BSM (port 2001) and SPAT (port 2004)
Certificate chain validation rejects expired and revoked pseudonym certs
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement SAE J2735-2023 ASN.1 codec: generate C code from J2735 ASN.1 schema using asn1c or equivalent, producing encode/decode functions for BasicSafetyMessage, SPAT, MapData, SignalRequestMessage, SignalStatusMessage, and PersonalSafetyMessage. Enforce J2735 data element constraints (range checks, optional field handling).
acceptance_criteria: Generated codec compiles with zero warnings under -Wall -Wextra
BSM encode produces byte-identical output to J2735 reference test vectors
Fuzz test (AFL++ 24h run) produces no crashes or memory corruption
Constraint checks reject out-of-range values for speed (0..8191), heading (0..28800), and position (-1800000000..1800000000)
Codec throughput ≥10,000 BSM encode/decode cycles/second on target SoC
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement the Intersection Collision Warning (ICW) application: consume incoming BSMs and local vehicle state (GPS, speed, heading from GNSS/IMU fusion), compute Time-to-Intersection (TTI) and Time-to-Collision (TTC), apply J2945/1 prediction model, and trigger audible/visual HMI alert when TTC < configurable threshold.
acceptance_criteria: TTC computation produces result within 10 ms of BSM receipt (profiled on target)
Map-matching algorithm correctly identifies vehicle lane on 10 stored intersection MAPs
False positive rate ≤2% on ICW validation scenario dataset (100 scenarios)
False negative rate ≤0.1% (missed collision warning) on same dataset
HMI alert fires within 100 ms E2E from BSM over-the-air receive (timestamped log)
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement the Emergency Vehicle Preemption (EVP) application: transmit SRM (Signal Request Message) from emergency vehicle OBU with strategic role set to emergency, receive SSM (Signal Status Message) from RSU, confirm preemption grant, and expose CAN signal to in-vehicle siren/light control ECU. RSU side: receive SRM, grant preemption via NTCIP 1202 TSC interface.
acceptance_criteria: SRM transmitted at 10 Hz when emergency vehicle within 300 m of intersection
SSM received and grant confirmed within 500 ms of SRM transmission (wireshark capture)
NTCIP 1202 SET preemption command issued to simulated TSC and ACKed
CAN signal EVP_grant transitions HIGH within 50 ms of SSM grant receipt
Preemption cancelled automatically when emergency vehicle clears intersection (MAP boundary)
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement the cooperative platooning application per SAE J3161/3 and SAE J2945/4: leader broadcasts BSM with TravelerDataFrame platooning extension, followers subscribe, implement CACC (Cooperative Adaptive Cruise Control) using V2X headway + radar fusion, inter-vehicle gap control PID, and platoon join/leave state machine.
acceptance_criteria: Platoon join state machine transitions correctly through all states in simulation
CACC gap error RMS ≤0.3 m at 80 km/h with 3-vehicle platoon (HIL test log)
Emergency dissolve triggered within 200 ms of lead vehicle emergency braking event
V2X + radar fusion reduces gap oscillation by ≥30% vs radar-only baseline (data log)
BSM platooning extension fields encode/decode correctly per J3161 Annex
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Implement GNSS/IMU position and heading fusion engine: integrate GNSS (u-blox F9P or equivalent RTK-capable) with 6-DOF IMU via extended Kalman filter. Output fused position (lat/lon/alt), heading, speed, and confidence ellipse fields used in BSM DF_PositionConfidenceSet and DE_Heading.
acceptance_criteria: EKF converges within 30 seconds of cold start (position error ≤2 m)
Fused heading accuracy ≤0.5° RMS in dynamic slalom test (vs reference INS)
BSM PositionConfidenceSet fields accurately reflect current HDOP-derived uncertainty
GNSS-denied tunnel mode (IMU dead-reckoning) maintains ≤5 m drift over 30 s
Output rate 100 Hz from IMU, 10 Hz from GNSS, fused at 100 Hz
AUTOSAR compliance checked
MISRA C violations zero

plan.description: Build a multi-vehicle V2X simulation environment using ns-3 with WAVE / 802.11p module and Sumo traffic simulator (TraCI interface). Model channel fading (WINNER+ B1 urban canyon model), packet error rate vs distance, and congestion control (ETSI TS 102 687 DCC). Simulate ICW, EVP, and platooning scenarios with 10–50 vehicles.
acceptance_criteria: ns-3 simulation builds and runs 10-vehicle ICW scenario without crashes
PDR ≥90% at 150 m inter-vehicle distance in B1 channel model (simulation log)
E2E BSM latency P99 ≤100 ms at 30 vehicles/km density
DCC algorithm reduces CBR from 0.65 to ≤0.60 under 50-vehicle load
Simulation outputs Wireshark-compatible PCAP for offline protocol analysis

plan.description: Build an RF channel emulator test bench: configure a Spirent or Rohde & Schwarz AWGN/fading channel emulator to inject 802.11p and C-V2X waveforms between two OBU development boards. Script scenarios for multi-path fading, Doppler shift at highway speeds, and jamming interference. Verify receiver sensitivity and demodulation performance.
acceptance_criteria: PER ≤1% at SNR ≥10 dB for both DSRC and C-V2X waveforms
Receiver sensitivity ≤-85 dBm for DSRC MCS-0 (BPSK 1/2)
Doppler tolerance verified at vehicle closure rates up to 200 km/h (±2.8 kHz)
CW jammer at -10 dBm degrades PER gracefully (≤30%) without firmware crash
All test scripts version-controlled and run headlessly in CI via VISA/SCPI

plan.description: Build the RSU (Roadside Unit) backend service: Python/FastAPI daemon that interfaces with the WAVE stack via UDP socket, manages MAP/SPAT message broadcast scheduling, handles SRM/SSM EVP logic, connects to traffic signal controller via NTCIP 1202 SNMP, and exposes a REST management API for RSU configuration and monitoring.
acceptance_criteria: RSU service starts and connects to WAVE stack UDP socket within 5 seconds
SPAT broadcast at exactly 10 Hz verified by Wireshark capture (±5% jitter tolerance)
NTCIP 1202 preemption SNMP SET acknowledged by simulated TSC within 200 ms
REST /rsu/status returns 200 with signal phase state and connected vehicle count
Service restarts automatically on crash (systemd unit with Restart=on-failure)

plan.description: Build the V2X cloud backend: data ingestion pipeline consuming BSM/SPAT/MAP messages from RSU fleet via MQTT broker (Eclipse Mosquitto), storing in time-series DB (InfluxDB), providing REST endpoints for fleet analytics, misbehavior detection reporting, and certificate revocation list (CRL) push to SCMS.
acceptance_criteria: Cloud backend ingests 1,000 BSMs/second without message loss (load test with k6)
BSM data stored in InfluxDB with lat/lon/speed fields queryable by vehicle pseudonym
Misbehavior detection flags vehicles with position jumps >50 m/s (plausibility check)
CRL push to SCMS REST API completes within 30 seconds of misbehavior report
MQTT broker TLS enabled with client certificate authentication

plan.description: Build the V2X operations dashboard: React + TypeScript SPA displaying a live map (MapLibre GL JS) with vehicle positions (BSM feed), intersection phase state (SPAT visualization), EVP active preemptions, platoon formations, and system health metrics. Include RSU management and alert triage UI.
acceptance_criteria: Live map renders 100 vehicle icons at 10 Hz update rate with ≤16 ms frame time
SPAT phase ring visualization shows correct phase (green/yellow/red) for each movement
EVP active preemption indicator appears within 500 ms of SSM grant (WebSocket event)
Platoon view draws convoy lines connecting leader and followers by platoon ID
RSU management page allows MAP/SPAT config upload and shows broadcast status

plan.description: Design and document the full V2X system API surface: OBU REST/gRPC management API, RSU REST management API, cloud backend REST API, and WebSocket streaming API for the dashboard. Produce OpenAPI 3.1 specification for all REST endpoints. Define Protobuf schemas for OBU↔Cloud telemetry.
acceptance_criteria: OpenAPI spec validates against official schema validator with zero errors
All REST endpoints have request/response examples and error schema defined
Protobuf schemas compile for Python and C targets without warnings
API versioning strategy (URI /v1/) documented with deprecation policy
Authentication scheme (OAuth2 + mTLS) documented for all endpoints

plan.description: Design InfluxDB schema (measurements, tags, fields) for BSM telemetry, SPAT phase history, EVP event log, and platoon session log. Design PostgreSQL schema for RSU fleet registry, intersection MAP registry, certificate pseudonym mapping, and misbehavior detection case management.
acceptance_criteria: InfluxDB schema defined with appropriate tag keys for filtering by RSU and vehicle
PostgreSQL migration scripts produce all tables with FK constraints and indexes
Retention policy downsample task (Flux task) aggregates BSM to 1-min rollup correctly
Query: latest position for all active vehicles returns in ≤50 ms with 10k vehicle dataset
Schema migration reversible (up.sql + down.sql provided)

plan.description: Design and implement HIL (Hardware-in-the-Loop) test harness for OBU firmware: connect OBU dev board to PC via JTAG (for coverage instrumentation) and RF test port, inject synthetic BSM/SPAT/MAP packets via software-defined radio (USRP or HackRF), and verify ICW alert timing, EVP state machine, and platooning gap control on real hardware.
acceptance_criteria: HIL test harness injects synthetic BSMs at 10 Hz and OBU receives without packet loss
ICW alert fires within 100 ms of SDR-injected collision-course BSM (timestamp delta logged)
EVP state machine test: SRM→SSM grant→CAN signal HIGH sequence verified in ≤500 ms
Platooning gap control HIL test achieves RMS gap error ≤0.5 m with SDR-simulated lead vehicle
Firmware code coverage ≥80% for icw_app.c, evp_obu.c, platoon_app.c after HIL suite run

plan.description: Write firmware unit tests for all RTOS tasks and HAL modules using Unity + CMock framework. Cover J2735 codec edge cases, EKF fusion state transitions, ICW TTC boundary conditions, EVP state machine transitions, and WAVE security certificate validation. Run in CI on host (x86 cross-compiled to run natively) and on target via JTAG.
acceptance_criteria: All unit test suites compile and pass with zero failures on x86 host
J2735 codec tests cover ≥15 boundary conditions including out-of-range rejection
EKF unit test verifies filter convergence with known synthetic trajectory (RMSE ≤0.3 m)
EVP FSM test covers all 8 state transitions with mock SRM/SSM inputs
Certificate validation test rejects 5 invalid cert types (expired, wrong PSID, bad signature, revoked, future-dated)

plan.description: Write integration tests for the RSU backend service and cloud backend: end-to-end BSM ingest via UDP→MQTT→InfluxDB, SPAT broadcast cycle, EVP SRM→SSM→NTCIP 1202→CAN pipeline, and misbehavior detection trigger. Use pytest with Docker Compose test environment spinning up real Mosquitto + InfluxDB + PostgreSQL.
acceptance_criteria: BSM injected via UDP appears in InfluxDB within 500 ms (asserted in test)
SPAT broadcast test verifies exactly 10 messages/second over 10-second window
EVP pipeline test confirms NTCIP 1202 SNMP SET fired within 500 ms of SRM receipt
Misbehavior detection test triggers CRL report for vehicle with 100 m position jump
All 200+ integration tests pass in CI Docker Compose environment within 5 minutes

plan.description: Execute end-to-end system validation scenarios in the ns-3 + SUMO simulation environment from Step 14 with actual firmware application logic compiled for Linux (x86 POSIX target). Validate J2945/1 ICW warning rates, J3082 EVP preemption success rate, J2945/4 platooning stability, and channel congestion behavior under varying vehicle densities.
acceptance_criteria: ICW true positive rate ≥99.5% across 100 NGSIM intersection scenarios
EVP preemption success rate ≥99% at distances ≤300 m in clear channel
Platoon string stability criterion: spacing error does not amplify upstream (L∞ norm < 1)
CBR remains ≤0.6 at 50 vehicles/km with DCC active
System test report generated with pass/fail for each KPI threshold

plan.description: Set up CI/CD pipeline: GitHub Actions workflows for firmware build + unit test + static analysis (MISRA-C via cppcheck + pc-lint), RSU/cloud backend test + Docker image build, frontend lint + build + Cypress E2E, ns-3 simulation smoke test, and nightly full system test suite. Artifact promotion via semantic versioning tags.
acceptance_criteria: PR pipeline completes in ≤15 minutes including all firmware unit tests
MISRA-C violation check fails PR if any new Rule 13.1, 14.2, or 15.5 violations introduced
Docker images tagged with git SHA and pushed to GHCR on merge to main
Nightly HIL regression runs 50 scenarios autonomously and posts pass/fail Slack summary
Release artifacts include signed firmware binary, SBOM.json, and OpenAPI spec

plan.description: Define infrastructure-as-code for cloud backend deployment: Kubernetes manifests (or Helm chart) for RSU backend, cloud ingest service, InfluxDB, PostgreSQL, Mosquitto MQTT broker, and Grafana dashboard. Include horizontal pod autoscaler for ingest service, persistent volumes for databases, and network policies for mTLS enforcement.
acceptance_criteria: helm install deploys all components to a KIND cluster without errors
HPA scales cloud-backend from 2 to 5 replicas when CPU >70% (load test triggered)
PersistentVolumeClaims bound and InfluxDB survives pod restart with data intact
Network policy blocks pod-to-pod traffic except defined service dependencies
Grafana dashboard displays BSM ingest rate, EVP events/hour, and system health

plan.description: Prepare AUTOSAR Classic SWC (Software Component) architecture description in ARXML for the OBU application layer: define ICW_SWC, EVP_SWC, Platoon_SWC with port interfaces, runnable entities (10 ms, 100 ms tasks), and inter-runnable variables. Map to AUTOSAR OS tasks and COM signals. Validate against AUTOSAR schema.
acceptance_criteria: ARXML validates against AUTOSAR R22-11 schema with zero errors
All port interfaces between SWCs have matching P-Port and R-Port definitions
Runnable entities assigned to correct OS tasks with deadlines specified
COM signals mapped between WAVE_COM_SWC and ICW/EVP/Platoon SWCs
Memory footprint estimate per SWC derived from ARXML and within flash budget

plan.description: Produce ISO 26262 Part 6 (software) evidence artifacts: software architecture description with ASIL decomposition, coding guidelines compliance report (MISRA-C 2012 deviation list), software unit test report, and software integration test report. Map to DHF sections from Step 3.
acceptance_criteria: SW architecture description covers all SWCs with ASIL assignment and decomposition rationale
MISRA-C deviation list has ≤10 deviations, each with category, rationale, and reviewer signature
SW unit test report shows ≥80% MC/DC coverage for ASIL-C EVP_SWC modules
SW integration test report covers all SWC interface interactions
All artifacts cross-referenced in traceability matrix from Step 3

plan.description: Implement a V2X channel health monitoring agent using the coordinator pattern: a coordinator agent spawns specialist sub-agents for DSRC congestion analysis (CBR monitoring), C-V2X HARQ failure rate analysis, and misbehavior detection review. Agent reads InfluxDB metrics, proposes DCC parameter adjustments or misbehavior reports, and requires human approval before any change is executed.

sub_agents.dsrc_agent: monitors CBR, proposes DCC parameter changes
sub_agents.cv2x_agent: monitors HARQ failure rate, proposes resource pool adjustments
sub_agents.mbd_agent: reviews misbehavior detection reports, proposes CRL submissions

acceptance_criteria: Coordinator agent decomposes channel health task and routes to correct sub-agent
DSRC agent generates DCC parameter proposal when CBR >0.6 (logged in audit trail)
MBD agent proposal for CRL submission created within 60 seconds of misbehavior flag
All proposals appear in SAGE approvals queue before any action is taken
Rejection of any proposal stores feedback in vector memory for future improvement

plan.description: Design HMI (Human-Machine Interface) specifications for the in-vehicle V2X alert system: visual and audible alert hierarchy for ICW (approaching collision), EVP (yield to emergency vehicle), and platooning status (gap, speed, leader ID). Comply with SAE J2945/9 HMI guidelines and ISO 15008 visual distraction standards.
acceptance_criteria: Wireframes cover all 6 alert states with correct color, icon, and text per J2945/9
Audible chime sequences differentiated: ICW ≥600 Hz, EVP 400 Hz, platoon 800 Hz
Alert display time meets ISO 15008: ≤2 seconds glance time to comprehend status
Distraction assessment checklist completed with all items PASS
HMI spec reviewed and approved by ux_designer and safety_engineer

plan.description: Develop V&V test plan and execute functional qualification testing (OQ) for the complete V2X system against all SRs from Step 1. Produce test execution report with pass/fail status per SR. Include radio conformance testing checklist referencing FCC Part 90 Subpart M (DSRC) and ETSI EN 302 663 (ITS-G5).
acceptance_criteria: Test plan covers 100% of SRs with at least one test case per SR
OQ execution report shows ≥95% pass rate with all critical safety SRs passing
Conformance checklist completed for FCC Part 90.377 and ETSI EN 302 663 mandatory tests
All failures have linked defect tickets with severity classification
OQ report signed by qa_engineer and regulatory_specialist

plan.description: Produce final documentation package: System Architecture Document (SAD) covering OBU + RSU + cloud tiers, ICD (Interface Control Document) for all external interfaces (NTCIP 1202, SCMS, J2735 air interface), OBU firmware developer guide, RSU operator manual, and API reference generated from OpenAPI spec.
acceptance_criteria: SAD includes architecture diagrams for all three tiers with numbered interface lines
ICD covers all 8 external interfaces with message format, encoding, and timing specs
Doxygen-generated firmware API reference covers all public headers with no undocumented functions
RSU operator manual includes step-by-step MAP/SPAT configuration and troubleshooting guide
All documents published to GitHub Pages and linked from repo README

flaws: ASIL assignments are predefined as inputs to HARA (Step 2 payload lists 'ASIL-B for ICW', 'ASIL-C for EVP' before the hazard analysis runs). ASIL is an OUTPUT of HARA, not a starting assumption. This inverts the ISO 26262 Part 3 process and invalidates the safety case.

AUTOSAR Classic R22-11 is used in Step 27 (SWC architecture, AUTOSAR OS tasks, RTE) but AUTOSAR Adaptive R23-11 is set up in Step 5. These are architecturally incompatible. Classic uses AUTOSAR OS/COM/RTE; Adaptive uses POSIX/ara::com/service-oriented communication. The plan never resolves which one the OBU actually uses or defines their boundary.

Platooning gap control at 80 km/h with 5 m target gap assigned ASIL-B. At those parameters, a spurious gap-close command or missed emergency dissolve is a rear-end collision at highway speed. ISO 26262 HARA would almost certainly yield ASIL-C or ASIL-D for the gap-control actuator path. ASIL-B is likely underclassified without documented rationale.

Step 7 implements C-V2X PC5 sidelink (3GPP TS 36.213/38.885) as a single firmware step with 7 acceptance criteria. PC5 mode 2 autonomous resource selection requires access to modem lower MAC/PHY via proprietary vendor API (Qualcomm QCA9150 SDK, Autotalks CRATON2). These SDKs are under NDA, have their own bugs, and typically take 3-6 months to integrate. The step treats it as equivalent complexity to the DSRC HAL.

Step 5 lists 'OpenV2X_reference' as the WAVE stack. OpenV2X is an early-stage open-source project that does not have production-grade IEEE 1609.3 WSMP, complete 1609.2 security, or validated ITS-G5 GeoNetworking. Step 8 then builds protocol layers *above* this stack. If OpenV2X is incomplete or non-conformant, Steps 8-12 are built on a broken foundation.

Steps 6 and 7 require physical hardware measurements (SPI ACK, oscilloscope GPIO toggle, SLSS lock timing) against specific boards (NXP S32G eval, Cohda MK6 modem). No step procures or provisions this hardware. The HIL test bench in Step 21 also assumes USRP B210 and SEGGER JLink are available. Hardware lead times in automotive can be 8-20 weeks.

SCMS integration is treated as a configuration item across Steps 4 and 8. There is no dedicated step for: enrollment certificate bootstrapping, butterfly key expansion for pseudonym provisioning, linkage authority interaction, or pseudonym certificate pre-loading onto the OBU. The CAMP SCMS REST API is referenced but its certificate lifecycle complexity (enrollment cert → pseudonym request → batch download → rotation) is not designed.

Step 24 treats the Linux POSIX x86 firmware port as a trivial variant: 'firmware application logic compiled for Linux.' Porting FreeRTOS-dependent code (RTOS task timing, queue semantics, ISR-safe APIs) to POSIX with correct timing behavior is a non-trivial effort. FreeRTOS POSIX port has known timing jitter that would invalidate latency acceptance criteria (100 ms E2E ICW) if not accounted for.

OTA firmware update (UNECE R156 SOTA requirement) is documented in Step 3 as a compliance artifact but never implemented. There is no step that builds the secure boot chain, firmware signing, delta update packaging, rollback mechanism, or update campaign management. This is a significant implementation gap for a production system.

Step 14 assumes the WINNER+ B1 channel model and ETSI TS 102 687 DCC algorithm are available in ns-3 3.40 out of the box. Neither is. B1 requires the COST 2100 or similar external module; DCC requires custom implementation. The simulation environment would need to be built first, but this is not captured as a sub-task.
suggestions: Split Step 2 into two phases: (a) hazard identification and S/E/C rating — producing a draft ASIL table — followed by (b) ASIL assignment and decomposition. Remove the predefined ASIL candidates from the payload and let HARA produce them.

Add an explicit architectural decision step (before Step 5) that resolves AUTOSAR Classic vs. Adaptive partitioning: which ECU runs which profile, what is the communication boundary, and which steps apply to which AUTOSAR variant. Without this, Steps 5, 6, 7, 27, and 28 are building on an undefined foundation.

Add a dedicated SCMS Integration step after Step 4 covering: enrollment certificate issuance flow, pseudonym certificate download batch size, certificate change triggers (location-based, time-based), and CRL/LCRL pull vs. push strategy. This is 2-3 weeks of work minimum and is load-bearing for all security acceptance criteria.

Add a Hardware Procurement and Lab Setup step (prerequisite to Steps 6, 7, 15, 21) listing all required hardware (OBU eval boards, Cohda/Autotalks modems, USRP, RF emulator, JTAG probes) with lead times and fallback simulation paths for each.

Add a FreeRTOS POSIX Simulation Layer step between Step 5 and Step 24 to explicitly design the FreeRTOS-to-POSIX abstraction, timing shim, and mock HAL layer used in co-simulation. Define which acceptance criteria are waived in simulation vs. required on target.

Elevate UNECE R156 OTA implementation to its own step: secure boot chain design, firmware image signing (Ed25519 or RSA-PSS), delta update packaging, bootloader handoff, rollback-on-failure, and integration with the SCMS for update campaign authorization.

Add a spectrum coordination sub-task to Step 5 or Step 6 addressing: the FCC 2020 5.9 GHz reallocation (DSRC now at 5.895–5.925 GHz), CEPT ECC Decision (19)01 for ITS-G5, and coexistence with Wi-Fi in the lower sub-band. Wrong channel configuration will fail FCC Part 90 conformance.

Step 12 CACC PID acceptance criterion (RMS gap error ≤0.3 m at 80 km/h) requires vehicle dynamics parameters that don't exist yet in this plan. Add a vehicle dynamics model identification step or scope the HIL test to a specific known vehicle model with documented transfer function.

Add explicit penetration testing execution to Step 4 or as a separate step. UNECE R155 Annex 5 requires demonstrated attack resistance, not just a threat model. A signed pentest report from a qualified tester is required for type approval — the threat model alone does not satisfy this.

Step 17 misbehavior detection threshold (position jump >50 m/s) only catches trivial spoofing. Add a Sybil attack detection algorithm specification (e.g., ETSI TS 103 759 Section 6 local MBD) and define how it handles low-and-slow spoofing, cooperative false data injection, and position falsification within plausible kinematic bounds.
missing: FCC Part 90 Subpart M and ETSI EN 302 663 type approval planning: engagement with accredited test lab, estimated timeline (6-18 months), and pre-compliance test strategy. Referenced as a checklist in Step 31 but not planned as an activity.

Pseudonym certificate change strategy: when/how the OBU rotates pseudonyms (per SAE J2945/1 Annex B), implications for platoon session continuity (platoon ID tied to certificate?), and privacy analysis showing unlinkability.

GNSS timing accuracy specification for C-V2X SLSS: PC5 mode 2 requires <1 μs synchronization accuracy. The plan mentions GNSS_primary but does not specify which GNSS timing signal (PPS output from u-blox F9T vs F9P position output) or the UTC timing chain to modem timing reference.

Vehicle dynamics model for CACC tuning: the PID gains in Step 12 cannot be specified without longitudinal vehicle model parameters (mass, drivetrain lag, brake actuator response time). Either a specific vehicle model must be selected or adaptive gain scheduling must be designed.

RF spectrum coexistence plan for DSRC + C-V2X hybrid OBU: concurrent operation on the same 5.9 GHz band requires time or frequency domain sharing arbitration not addressed in the radio abstraction layer design.

Certificate revocation propagation latency budget: CRL/delta-CRL propagation to OBUs in the field can take seconds to minutes. The security model must define the maximum acceptable revocation latency and how the system behaves during the window between revocation and CRL receipt.

Step 15 RF channel emulator hardware budget: R&S CMW500 costs $80,000-$150,000; Spirent Vertex is comparable. If this hardware is not available, Step 15's acceptance criteria (PER vs SNR curves, Doppler tolerance) have no path to completion.

NTCIP 1202 TSC simulator: Steps 11 and 23 both require a simulated Traffic Signal Controller responding to SNMP SET. No step creates or procures this simulator. Open-source TSC simulators (e.g., NTCIP-sim) are not production-grade.

Fail-operational behavior specification: what happens when the V2X radio fails mid-platoon? The emergency_dissolve state exists but the fail-safe behavior (fall back to radar-only CACC, controlled deceleration, driver takeover alert) is not specified as a requirement.
security_risks: wolfSSL 5.x ECDSA P-256 implementation: must be verified for constant-time execution on the target SoC to prevent timing side-channel attacks on private key operations. wolfSSL has had CVEs in this area (e.g., CVE-2022-25640). Step 8 specifies the library but no side-channel analysis is planned.

BSM pseudonym linkability: if pseudonym certificates are not changed frequently enough or change events are predictable (e.g., always at the same intersection), an attacker can correlate BSMs across certificates and track a vehicle. No privacy analysis or pseudonym change audit is in scope.

MQTT broker client certificate rotation: Step 17 specifies mTLS client certificates for RSU-to-cloud MQTT but does not address certificate lifecycle management for the broker's CA. A compromised RSU with a valid client cert can inject arbitrary BSMs into the cloud backend.

CRL injection attack: the cloud backend pushes CRLs to SCMS (Step 17). If an attacker can trigger a misbehavior report for a legitimate emergency vehicle, its pseudonym cert could be revoked, blocking EVP. The misbehavior detection threshold and reporting authorization are not hardened against adversarial inputs.

OBU firmware update signing key management: when UNECE R156 OTA is eventually implemented, the firmware signing key is the highest-value target in the entire system. HSM-backed key storage and key ceremony procedures are not mentioned anywhere.

GPS spoofing against the EKF: Step 13 implements GPS/IMU fusion but the EKF does not include GNSS spoofing detection. A spoofed GNSS signal that moves slowly within EKF innovation bounds will pass through to the BSM position field, enabling false ICW triggers or platoon disruption. GNSS spoofing detection (signal strength monitoring, clock drift analysis) is not in scope.

SCMS REST API authentication for CRL push (Step 17): if the cloud backend's SCMS integration uses API keys rather than mTLS + certificate-bound tokens, a compromised cloud backend credential enables unauthorized CRL submissions — effectively a denial-of-service against any vehicle's pseudonym cert.
final_review.summary: This is a technically sophisticated and architecturally comprehensive V2X plan that demonstrates genuine domain expertise across firmware, safety, security, and systems engineering. The dependency graph is correct, latency budgets are specific, and the acceptance criteria are measurable. However, it fails the 85+ threshold required for regulated automotive production on several fundamental grounds: ASIL assignments precede the HARA that should generate them (a process violation that would fail an ISO 26262 audit); AUTOSAR Classic and Adaptive are conflated without architectural resolution; the C-V2X PC5 implementation complexity is severely underestimated relative to the single step allocated; the WAVE stack dependency on OpenV2X is a production risk; and OTA firmware update — a hard UNECE R156 requirement — has no implementation step. Beyond these structural issues, SCMS certificate lifecycle, pseudonym privacy, GNSS spoofing defense, and type approval planning are missing. The plan would produce a capable research prototype or proof-of-concept at its current state, but requires fundamental rework in the safety process, AUTOSAR architecture, and compliance implementation before it could pass an automotive homologation audit.

flaws: ASIL assignments are predefined as inputs to HARA (Step 2 payload lists 'ASIL-B for ICW', 'ASIL-C for EVP' before the hazard analysis runs). ASIL is an OUTPUT of HARA, not a starting assumption. This inverts the ISO 26262 Part 3 process and invalidates the safety case.

AUTOSAR Classic R22-11 is used in Step 27 (SWC architecture, AUTOSAR OS tasks, RTE) but AUTOSAR Adaptive R23-11 is set up in Step 5. These are architecturally incompatible. Classic uses AUTOSAR OS/COM/RTE; Adaptive uses POSIX/ara::com/service-oriented communication. The plan never resolves which one the OBU actually uses or defines their boundary.

Platooning gap control at 80 km/h with 5 m target gap assigned ASIL-B. At those parameters, a spurious gap-close command or missed emergency dissolve is a rear-end collision at highway speed. ISO 26262 HARA would almost certainly yield ASIL-C or ASIL-D for the gap-control actuator path. ASIL-B is likely underclassified without documented rationale.

Step 7 implements C-V2X PC5 sidelink (3GPP TS 36.213/38.885) as a single firmware step with 7 acceptance criteria. PC5 mode 2 autonomous resource selection requires access to modem lower MAC/PHY via proprietary vendor API (Qualcomm QCA9150 SDK, Autotalks CRATON2). These SDKs are under NDA, have their own bugs, and typically take 3-6 months to integrate. The step treats it as equivalent complexity to the DSRC HAL.

Step 5 lists 'OpenV2X_reference' as the WAVE stack. OpenV2X is an early-stage open-source project that does not have production-grade IEEE 1609.3 WSMP, complete 1609.2 security, or validated ITS-G5 GeoNetworking. Step 8 then builds protocol layers *above* this stack. If OpenV2X is incomplete or non-conformant, Steps 8-12 are built on a broken foundation.

Steps 6 and 7 require physical hardware measurements (SPI ACK, oscilloscope GPIO toggle, SLSS lock timing) against specific boards (NXP S32G eval, Cohda MK6 modem). No step procures or provisions this hardware. The HIL test bench in Step 21 also assumes USRP B210 and SEGGER JLink are available. Hardware lead times in automotive can be 8-20 weeks.

SCMS integration is treated as a configuration item across Steps 4 and 8. There is no dedicated step for: enrollment certificate bootstrapping, butterfly key expansion for pseudonym provisioning, linkage authority interaction, or pseudonym certificate pre-loading onto the OBU. The CAMP SCMS REST API is referenced but its certificate lifecycle complexity (enrollment cert → pseudonym request → batch download → rotation) is not designed.

Step 24 treats the Linux POSIX x86 firmware port as a trivial variant: 'firmware application logic compiled for Linux.' Porting FreeRTOS-dependent code (RTOS task timing, queue semantics, ISR-safe APIs) to POSIX with correct timing behavior is a non-trivial effort. FreeRTOS POSIX port has known timing jitter that would invalidate latency acceptance criteria (100 ms E2E ICW) if not accounted for.

OTA firmware update (UNECE R156 SOTA requirement) is documented in Step 3 as a compliance artifact but never implemented. There is no step that builds the secure boot chain, firmware signing, delta update packaging, rollback mechanism, or update campaign management. This is a significant implementation gap for a production system.

Step 14 assumes the WINNER+ B1 channel model and ETSI TS 102 687 DCC algorithm are available in ns-3 3.40 out of the box. Neither is. B1 requires the COST 2100 or similar external module; DCC requires custom implementation. The simulation environment would need to be built first, but this is not captured as a sub-task.
suggestions: Split Step 2 into two phases: (a) hazard identification and S/E/C rating — producing a draft ASIL table — followed by (b) ASIL assignment and decomposition. Remove the predefined ASIL candidates from the payload and let HARA produce them.

Add an explicit architectural decision step (before Step 5) that resolves AUTOSAR Classic vs. Adaptive partitioning: which ECU runs which profile, what is the communication boundary, and which steps apply to which AUTOSAR variant. Without this, Steps 5, 6, 7, 27, and 28 are building on an undefined foundation.

Add a dedicated SCMS Integration step after Step 4 covering: enrollment certificate issuance flow, pseudonym certificate download batch size, certificate change triggers (location-based, time-based), and CRL/LCRL pull vs. push strategy. This is 2-3 weeks of work minimum and is load-bearing for all security acceptance criteria.

Add a Hardware Procurement and Lab Setup step (prerequisite to Steps 6, 7, 15, 21) listing all required hardware (OBU eval boards, Cohda/Autotalks modems, USRP, RF emulator, JTAG probes) with lead times and fallback simulation paths for each.

Add a FreeRTOS POSIX Simulation Layer step between Step 5 and Step 24 to explicitly design the FreeRTOS-to-POSIX abstraction, timing shim, and mock HAL layer used in co-simulation. Define which acceptance criteria are waived in simulation vs. required on target.

Elevate UNECE R156 OTA implementation to its own step: secure boot chain design, firmware image signing (Ed25519 or RSA-PSS), delta update packaging, bootloader handoff, rollback-on-failure, and integration with the SCMS for update campaign authorization.

Add a spectrum coordination sub-task to Step 5 or Step 6 addressing: the FCC 2020 5.9 GHz reallocation (DSRC now at 5.895–5.925 GHz), CEPT ECC Decision (19)01 for ITS-G5, and coexistence with Wi-Fi in the lower sub-band. Wrong channel configuration will fail FCC Part 90 conformance.

Step 12 CACC PID acceptance criterion (RMS gap error ≤0.3 m at 80 km/h) requires vehicle dynamics parameters that don't exist yet in this plan. Add a vehicle dynamics model identification step or scope the HIL test to a specific known vehicle model with documented transfer function.

Add explicit penetration testing execution to Step 4 or as a separate step. UNECE R155 Annex 5 requires demonstrated attack resistance, not just a threat model. A signed pentest report from a qualified tester is required for type approval — the threat model alone does not satisfy this.

Step 17 misbehavior detection threshold (position jump >50 m/s) only catches trivial spoofing. Add a Sybil attack detection algorithm specification (e.g., ETSI TS 103 759 Section 6 local MBD) and define how it handles low-and-slow spoofing, cooperative false data injection, and position falsification within plausible kinematic bounds.
missing: FCC Part 90 Subpart M and ETSI EN 302 663 type approval planning: engagement with accredited test lab, estimated timeline (6-18 months), and pre-compliance test strategy. Referenced as a checklist in Step 31 but not planned as an activity.

Pseudonym certificate change strategy: when/how the OBU rotates pseudonyms (per SAE J2945/1 Annex B), implications for platoon session continuity (platoon ID tied to certificate?), and privacy analysis showing unlinkability.

GNSS timing accuracy specification for C-V2X SLSS: PC5 mode 2 requires <1 μs synchronization accuracy. The plan mentions GNSS_primary but does not specify which GNSS timing signal (PPS output from u-blox F9T vs F9P position output) or the UTC timing chain to modem timing reference.

Vehicle dynamics model for CACC tuning: the PID gains in Step 12 cannot be specified without longitudinal vehicle model parameters (mass, drivetrain lag, brake actuator response time). Either a specific vehicle model must be selected or adaptive gain scheduling must be designed.

RF spectrum coexistence plan for DSRC + C-V2X hybrid OBU: concurrent operation on the same 5.9 GHz band requires time or frequency domain sharing arbitration not addressed in the radio abstraction layer design.

Certificate revocation propagation latency budget: CRL/delta-CRL propagation to OBUs in the field can take seconds to minutes. The security model must define the maximum acceptable revocation latency and how the system behaves during the window between revocation and CRL receipt.

Step 15 RF channel emulator hardware budget: R&S CMW500 costs $80,000-$150,000; Spirent Vertex is comparable. If this hardware is not available, Step 15's acceptance criteria (PER vs SNR curves, Doppler tolerance) have no path to completion.

NTCIP 1202 TSC simulator: Steps 11 and 23 both require a simulated Traffic Signal Controller responding to SNMP SET. No step creates or procures this simulator. Open-source TSC simulators (e.g., NTCIP-sim) are not production-grade.

Fail-operational behavior specification: what happens when the V2X radio fails mid-platoon? The emergency_dissolve state exists but the fail-safe behavior (fall back to radar-only CACC, controlled deceleration, driver takeover alert) is not specified as a requirement.
security_risks: wolfSSL 5.x ECDSA P-256 implementation: must be verified for constant-time execution on the target SoC to prevent timing side-channel attacks on private key operations. wolfSSL has had CVEs in this area (e.g., CVE-2022-25640). Step 8 specifies the library but no side-channel analysis is planned.

BSM pseudonym linkability: if pseudonym certificates are not changed frequently enough or change events are predictable (e.g., always at the same intersection), an attacker can correlate BSMs across certificates and track a vehicle. No privacy analysis or pseudonym change audit is in scope.

MQTT broker client certificate rotation: Step 17 specifies mTLS client certificates for RSU-to-cloud MQTT but does not address certificate lifecycle management for the broker's CA. A compromised RSU with a valid client cert can inject arbitrary BSMs into the cloud backend.

CRL injection attack: the cloud backend pushes CRLs to SCMS (Step 17). If an attacker can trigger a misbehavior report for a legitimate emergency vehicle, its pseudonym cert could be revoked, blocking EVP. The misbehavior detection threshold and reporting authorization are not hardened against adversarial inputs.

OBU firmware update signing key management: when UNECE R156 OTA is eventually implemented, the firmware signing key is the highest-value target in the entire system. HSM-backed key storage and key ceremony procedures are not mentioned anywhere.

GPS spoofing against the EKF: Step 13 implements GPS/IMU fusion but the EKF does not include GNSS spoofing detection. A spoofed GNSS signal that moves slowly within EKF innovation bounds will pass through to the BSM position field, enabling false ICW triggers or platoon disruption. GNSS spoofing detection (signal strength monitoring, clock drift analysis) is not in scope.

SCMS REST API authentication for CRL push (Step 17): if the cloud backend's SCMS integration uses API keys rather than mTLS + certificate-bound tokens, a compromised cloud backend credential enables unauthorized CRL submissions — effectively a denial-of-service against any vehicle's pseudonym cert.
history.summary: This is a technically sophisticated and architecturally comprehensive V2X plan that demonstrates genuine domain expertise across firmware, safety, security, and systems engineering. The dependency graph is correct, latency budgets are specific, and the acceptance criteria are measurable. However, it fails the 85+ threshold required for regulated automotive production on several fundamental grounds: ASIL assignments precede the HARA that should generate them (a process violation that would fail an ISO 26262 audit); AUTOSAR Classic and Adaptive are conflated without architectural resolution; the C-V2X PC5 implementation complexity is severely underestimated relative to the single step allocated; the WAVE stack dependency on OpenV2X is a production risk; and OTA firmware update — a hard UNECE R156 requirement — has no implementation step. Beyond these structural issues, SCMS certificate lifecycle, pseudonym privacy, GNSS spoofing defense, and type approval planning are missing. The plan would produce a capable research prototype or proof-of-concept at its current state, but requires fundamental rework in the safety process, AUTOSAR architecture, and compliance implementation before it could pass an automotive homologation audit.
